wine_history.name: Roman viticulture in the Rheingau
wine_history.description: Rome introduced viticulture to the Middle Rhine, and Roman settlements along the bend of the river between Hochheim and Lorch laid the groundwork for organised wine growing in what would become the Rheingau.
wine_history.evidence: Wikipedia and regional pages document Roman-era viticulture along the Rhine and Mosel that seeded the later monastic wine culture of the Rheingau.

wine_history.name: Cistercian founding of Kloster Eberbach, 1135
wine_history.description: In 1135 Bernard of Clairvaux founded the Cistercian abbey of Eberbach above Eltville, an event that gave the Rheingau its central viticultural anchor and a network of monks who would shape every steep slope on the river for the next 700 years.
wine_history.evidence: The Kloster Eberbach official site and the German Wikipedia article both record the 1135 Cistercian founding of the abbey.

wine_history.name: The Steinberg walled vineyard, from 1136
wine_history.description: From 1136 onward, Cistercian monks at Kloster Eberbach cleared a sloping hill above the abbey and built a high stone wall around it, creating the Steinberg, a Rheingau monopole that still produces Riesling for the Hessische Staatsweingueter today.
wine_history.evidence: Wikipedia and Schloss-Johannisberg's history pages document the Cistercian Steinberg as a walled Rheingau vineyard begun in the 12th century.

wine_history.name: Cistercian and monastic expansion of Rheingau vineyards
wine_history.description: Through the late Middle Ages, Eberbach and other monastic houses cleared and planted the steep south-facing slopes between Hochheim and Lorch, building cellars, presses and the riverbank wine trade that gave the Rheingau its identity as a Rhine wine country.
wine_history.evidence: Wein.plus and Kloster Eberbach materials document the medieval Cistercian monastic expansion of Rheingau viticulture across the steep Rhine slopes.

wine_history.name: Schloss Johannisberg replanted to Riesling, 1720
wine_history.description: In 1720 the prince-abbot Konstantin of Buttlar replanted Schloss Johannisberg entirely with Riesling, a documented decision to commit a single Rheingau estate exclusively to the grape that anchors the regional identity today.
wine_history.evidence: Schloss Johannisberg's own history page and the English Wikipedia article both record the 1720 replanting to Riesling under the Fulda prince-abbots.

wine_history.name: The Spaetlese courier at Schloss Johannisberg, 1775
wine_history.description: In 1775 a courier sent by the Fulda diocese to authorise the Schloss Johannisberg harvest arrived too late, and the over-ripe Riesling pressed from the delayed crop yielded an unusually rich late-harvest wine that Schloss Johannisberg records as the origin of its Spaetlese tradition.
wine_history.evidence: Schloss Johannisberg's official history page and the German wine pages on Spaetlese both record the 1775 delayed-courier story at Schloss Johannisberg.

wine_history.name: Secularisation of Kloster Eberbach, 1803
wine_history.description: Under Napoleonic secularisation, in 1803 Kloster Eberbach was dissolved as a monastery and its vineyards passed to the state of Nassau, then later to Hesse, forming the foundation of today's Hessische Staatsweingueter Kloster Eberbach.
wine_history.evidence: The Kloster Eberbach official site and the Wikipedia article both record the 1803 secularisation that transferred the abbey vineyards to the state.

wine_history.name: Geisenheim viticulture school founded, 1872
wine_history.description: In 1872 Eduard von Lade founded a teaching and research institute for fruit growing and viticulture at Geisenheim, the seed of today's Hochschule Geisenheim University, the German-speaking world's central wine and oenology research school.
wine_history.evidence: The Hochschule Geisenheim University website and the German Wikipedia article both record the 1872 founding of the Geisenheim Lehranstalt by Eduard von Lade.

wine_history.name: The 1971 German Wine Law
wine_history.description: The 1971 German Wine Law replaced centuries of single-site naming with a national Praedikat ladder based on must-weight, bundling many smaller historic sites into larger Grosslagen and reshaping the way every Rheingau bottle was labelled.
wine_history.evidence: The German Wikipedia article on Praedikat wine and Cork & Curve's regional sources document the structural change introduced by the 1971 law.

wine_history.name: VDP roots and the prestige-estate association
wine_history.description: The VDP grew from earlier 19th-century auction associations into the national Verband Deutscher Praedikatsweingueter, with the Rheingau as a founding regional chapter pushing site quality, dry styles and tighter classification across the late 20th century.
wine_history.evidence: The Wikipedia article on the VDP and the VDP website document the early 20th-century founding and the Rheingau chapter's role in the modern association.

wine_history.name: Charta Rheingau and Erstes Gewaechs, 1999
wine_history.description: From a 1984 dry-Riesling charter, Rheingau growers formalised Erstes Gewaechs in 1999, a regional dry top-tier classification keyed to mapped premium sites that became a model for the later national Grosses Gewaechs system.
wine_history.evidence: The German Wikipedia article on Erstes Gewaechs documents the Charta Rheingau initiative and the 1999 launch of the regional Erstes Gewaechs classification.

wine_history.name: VDP Grosses Gewaechs introduced, 2002
wine_history.description: In 2002 the VDP introduced Grosses Gewaechs nationally, a dry top-tier bottling from classified Grosse Lage sites, anchoring Rheingau dry Rieslings within a German-wide quality grammar.
wine_history.evidence: The Wikipedia article on Grosses Gewaechs and the VDP site document the 2002 launch of the national dry top-tier classification.

wine_history.name: VDP four-tier classification, 2012
wine_history.description: In 2012 the VDP consolidated its four-tier classification of Gutswein, Ortswein, Erste Lage and Grosse Lage, the framework most Rheingau VDP estates now use to organise their dry and praedikat ranges.
wine_history.evidence: The VDP website and the Wikipedia article on the VDP document the 2012 four-tier classification consolidation.

wine_history.name: Peter Jakob Kuhn turns biodynamic
wine_history.description: Peter Jakob Kuhn at Oestrich converted to biodynamic farming in the early 2000s and brought Demeter-certified Rheingau Riesling onto natural and fine-wine lists internationally, pulling other Rheingau growers toward certified organic and biodynamic farming.
wine_history.evidence: The Peter Jakob Kuhn estate site and the Demeter producer pages document the biodynamic conversion of the Oestrich estate.

wine_history.name: Spaetburgunder at Assmannshausen
wine_history.description: At the western edge of the Rheingau, Assmannshausen has grown Spaetburgunder on steep red-slate slopes since at least the 12th century, and its state-owned Domaene and family estates still bottle red-slate Pinot Noir from the Hoellenberg.
wine_history.evidence: The German Wikipedia article on Assmannshausen and Wein.plus regional pages document the historic Spaetburgunder tradition on the village's red-slate slopes.

wine_history.name: Climate-driven shift toward dry Riesling
wine_history.description: Warmer growing seasons since the 1990s have pushed Rheingau harvest dates earlier and raised ripeness levels, which growers have channelled into fully dry Riesling and GG-style bottlings alongside the historic off-dry and Spaetlese tradition.
wine_history.evidence: The Wein.plus regional pages and the Wikipedia article on the Rheingau document the contemporary shift toward dry and GG-style Riesling under warmer conditions.
